name: AV Dissociation (Isorhythmic)
identifyBy: P waves and QRS complexes march independently at nearly the same rate
P waves appear to 'walk through' the QRS - sometimes before, sometimes within, sometimes after
Rate of sinus P waves ≈ rate of junctional/ventricular QRS complexes
No fixed PR interval - varies as P and QRS drift in and out of phase
Capture beats may occur when P wave conducts - confirms functional AV conduction

measurements.rate: Both atrial and ventricular rates 60–100 bpm (nearly equal)
measurements.pWaves: Present but unrelated to QRS - march through

management: Often benign - frequently transient and self-terminating
Occurs in: inferior MI (enhanced junctional automaticity), digoxin toxicity, post-cardiac surgery
If junctional rate suppresses sinus: treat with Atropine to increase sinus rate
Do not confuse with complete heart block - in AV dissociation, AV conduction is FUNCTIONAL (capture beats possible)
pearls: AV dissociation ≠ complete heart block - the key difference: in AV dissociation, P waves CAN conduct when timing is right (capture beats); in CHB, NO P waves ever conduct
Isorhythmic = both rates are approximately equal - the two rhythms are in synchrony but not coupled
Enhanced junctional automaticity in inferior MI: junctional rate increases to match sinus rate - creates isorhythmic dissociation
Capture beats (short PR, conducted sinus beat): the diagnostic proof that AV conduction is intact
differentials: Complete heart block (P waves never conduct - no capture beats)
Accelerated junctional tachycardia (rate >60 bpm, AV dissociation at faster rate)

qaNotes: Independent atrial rhythm close to the ventricular rate with drifting P/QRS relationship.
